Title 13 — Parks & Recreation Plain-Language Service Free · no account

A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 13 (Parks & Recreation) — Chapter 13.04A Parks & Recreational Facilities: how to get a park permit, the rules for camping and pavilion/facility use, and the general park prohibitions. Administered by the Department of Parks & Recreation. Sections marked expanding are being filled in; nothing here is legal advice.

★ Parcel lookup live data

Optional context — enter a Maui County TMK to see designations from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. For an actual park permit or facility reservation, use the County's Park Permits page (linked in the result).

Tip: type the digits of your TMK (zone-section-plat-parcel). Don't know it? Open the State Land Use Viewer ↗ to find it on a map.

Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Park permits: County Park Permits. No data is stored or fabricated. park-boundary overlay

1 · Get a park permit sourced

Any activity, event, class, or program in a County park or facility needs a permit from the department first (Ch. 13.04A, Art. III).

  1. Pick your permit type
    Choose the right permit for your activity (camping, event, class, commercial ocean recreation, general use — see the table below).
    Expense: none to choose · paid by: you (time)
  2. Apply to Parks & Recreation
    Submit the written application to the Department of Parks & Recreation before the activity; camping applicants must be 18 or older.
    Expense: permit fee · paid by: applicant
  3. Carry your permit
    Keep a copy of the approved permit with you during the activity (required while camping).
    Expense: comply with conditions · paid by: permit holder
⚖️ Position — James Langford (analysis, not law; a stance beside the rule)Parks are the people's shared ʻāina. Permits should protect access and the land, not ration it — keep resident, cultural, and family use easy and low-cost, and reserve fees and limits for commercial or high-impact uses.

Maui County Code Ch. 13.04A, Art. III (Permits) + County Park Permits. online reservation portal + fee schedule

2 · Park permit types sourced

The permits authorized under Ch. 13.04A.

PermitForNote
Camping permitOvernight camping at a designated park18+; max 4 consecutive days per site
Special events permitEvents in a park/facilityapply in advance
Community class permitClasses/programsrecurring use
General use permitOther organized use—
Commercial ocean recreational activityCommercial ocean operatorsregulated
Government permitGovernment activities—

Maui County Code Ch. 13.04A (Parks & Recreational Facilities): camping §13.04A.200/.210, permits Art. III, prohibitions §13.04A.070. per-permit fees + reservation windows

3 · Who pays — the charter lens analysis

Labeled analysis — parks read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.

Maui County Charter — lensUnder the Maui County Charter, the Department of Parks & Recreation maintains parks and facilities and permits their use; fees offset maintenance and limit conflicts between uses. The Charter frames parks as public assets the department stewards.
Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) — lensUnder the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, the parks, shoreline, and gathering places are communal ʻāina held for all generations; access for residents, cultural practice, and ʻohana is a birthright. The lens favors free or low-cost resident access and pricing the burden onto commercial users who profit from the commons.
⚖️ Position — analysis, not lawKeep the parks open and cheap for the people; charge the commercial operators who monetize them. Permits should guard the ʻāina and resolve conflicts, not gate residents out of their own commons. Same title, read two ways.
Free now (no account): this page, the parcel lookup, the permit navigator, and the permit-type table are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): a permit-readiness checklist and an AI parks assistant will be verification-gated via the free govOS beta portal (identity verified with Stripe Identity — free, no charge); only verified-status is stored, never the raw ID. No secret keys (environment references only); any permit payment happens on the County's own system.
govOS · Kilo Aupuni · 12 Stones Global · free public service · sourced to Maui County Code Title 13 (Parks & Recreation), Ch. 13.04A via Municode + County of Maui Department of Parks & Recreation + Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS · first version 2026-06-18, expanding · Built by the County Service Module Engine (config + sourced content — no bespoke page) · not legal advice · public records only · hands off to the County's permit system.
